coredata.dc:title: Polyaniline-derived lotus-pod nano-hybrid incorporated luminescent N-CDs combined with novel co-reaction accelerator for ultra-sensitive electrochemiluminescence detection of trenbolone
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Yanshuo
Wang, Huan
Wang, Caiyu
Zhang, Baojian
Ma, Hongmin
Wu, Dan
Ren, Xiang
Li, Yuyang
Wei, Qin
coredata.dc:description: As an environmental steroidal contaminant, the widespread use of trenbolone (TB) and its metabolic emissions caused serious harm to the endocrine, cardiovascular, and mental health systems. The application of electrochemiluminescence (ECL) in environmental detection was contingent upon its high sensitivity, extensive dynamic range, and superior selectivity. Inspired by the lotus pod, a new material based on the lotus pod stabilization platform wrapped with N-CDs in polyaniline-derived polymers was designed to improve the ECL intensity significantly. PANI/N-CDs were prepared utilizing a solvothermal method based on polyaniline (PANI) and N-CDs with N, N-dimethylformamide as the solvent. This work constructed a novel ECL biosensor for TB detection utilizing CeO2/NiCo-LDH@Au as a co-reaction accelerator and PANI/N-CDs as the ECL probe. The CeO2/NiCo-LDH exhibited enhanced reaction acceleration and its nanoflower-like structure offered more binding sites for the immobilization of Au NPs and antibodies. The biosensor exhibited a response range of 0.1 pg/mL to 50.0 ng/mL and the limit of detection (LOD) of 68.4 fg/mL. The method realizes the ultra-sensitive detection of trenbolone, which broadens the application of the nanomaterial and provides a new platform for the detection of environmental pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PANI/N-CDs
CeO2/NiCo-LDH
Co-reaction accelerator
Trenbolone
Electrochemiluminescence
Biosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A-B) N-CDs and (C-D) PANI/N-CDs. SEM images of (E) CeO2, (F) NiCo-LDH, and (G) CeO2/NiCo-LDH.
(A) Full XPS spectra of polyaniline lotus derivatives. (B)-(D) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s of PANI/N-CDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (A) Ni 2p, (B) O 1 s, (C) Ce 3d, and (D) Co 2p of CeO2/NiCo-LDH.
(A) ECL responses of sensors at different TB concentrations. (B) Corresponding calibration curve.
(A) Stability, (B) store stability, (C) reproducibility, and (D) selectivity of ECL sensors.
Preparation of CeO2/NiCo-LDH@Au-Ab1 and PANI/N-CDs-Ab2, and construction diagram of ECL sensor.

head.ce:title: Polyaniline-derived lotus-pod nano-hybrid incorporated luminescent N-CDs combined with novel co-reaction accelerator for ultra-sensitive electrochemiluminescence detection of trenbolone

School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an environmental steroidal contaminant, the widespread use of trenbolone (TB) and its metabolic emissions caused serious harm to the endocrine, cardiovascular, and mental health systems. The application of electrochemiluminescence (ECL) in environmental detection was contingent upon its high sensitivity, extensive dynamic range, and superior selectivity. Inspired by the lotus pod, a new material based on the lotus pod stabilization platform wrapped with N-CDs in polyaniline-derived polymers was designed to improve the ECL intensity significantly. PANI/N-CDs were prepared utilizing a solvothermal method based on polyaniline (PANI) and N-CDs with N, N-dimethylformamide as the solvent. This work constructed a novel ECL biosensor for TB detection utilizing CeO2/NiCo-LDH@Au as a co-reaction accelerator and PANI/N-CDs as the ECL probe. The CeO2/NiCo-LDH exhibited enhanced reaction acceleration and its nanoflower-like structure offered more binding sites for the immobilization of Au NPs and antibodies. The biosensor exhibited a response range of 0.1 pg/mL to 50.0 ng/mL and the limit of detection (LOD) of 68.4 fg/mL. The method realizes the ultra-sensitive detection of trenbolone, which broadens the application of the nanomaterial and provides a new platform for the detection of environmental pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) boasts advantages such as rapid response [1], broad application range [2], and exceptional stability [3], making it a highly desirable technology in various fields. Among numerous nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) stand out due to their unique properties, remarkable stability, and biocompatibility, demonstrating vast application potential and significant research value [4]. However, the limitations of traditional CDs, namely low luminescence intensity and high excitation potential, somewhat hinder their further development in high-sensitivity detection [5]. To address this challenge, doping CDs with other elements, especially non-metals, offers a promising solution for enhancing ECL intensity, stability, and controllability [6,7]. It is noteworthy that in terms of biocompatibility and stability, Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are an effective way to optimize the surface state of CDs to improve ECL performance [8,9].

Polyaniline (PANI) is an extraordinarily high molecular weight compound with unique electrical and optical properties [10,11]. Its rare conjugated double bond system and low cost make it stand out in various applications [12,13]. The PANI-CDs system has shown extensive potential, including electrocatalysis, energy storage and conversion, anticorrosion materials, and photoelectric materials [14–16]. Although the research of PANI/N-CDs in the field of ECL is still relatively limited, it has broad development prospects in the field of ECL with its superior performance [17,18]. This system possesses the potential for developing highly sensitive and stable biosensors, offering powerful technical support to fields such as bioanalysis and environmental monitoring.

Layered double hydroxides (LDH) are a special category of inorganic materials, formed by tightly interwoven positively charged layers and anions, which possess abundant active sites, exceptional catalytic properties, and remarkable stability [19–21]. The low conductivity of LDH prevents electron transfer, resulting in a weakened signal intensity [22]. To tackle this limitation, the researchers used traditional methods such as precipitation and sol–gel techniques to carefully control morphology, particle size, and crystallinity, thereby optimizing performance [23,24]. In this study, a novel nanoflower-like NiCo-LDH was synthesized, which not only improved the structural stability of the materials and provided a larger specific surface area, but also significantly improved the accessibility of the active site [25]. The NiCo-LDH nanoflower structure significantly accelerated the rate of electron transfer and facilitated the efficient generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), while increasing the intensity of the ECL signal [26]. Moreover, this work innovatively incorporated cerium oxide (CeO2) and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs), which formed a notable ternary synergistic effect. CeO2, renowned for its potent catalytic activity and stability, further promoted the generation of ROS. Meanwhile, with the excellent electrical conductivity of Au NPs, the electron transfer pathway was improved and the charge transfer process was enhanced, which significantly amplified the ECL intensity [27–29].

Herein, a novel biosensor using hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as a co-reaction agent, PANI/N-CDs with the morphology of lotus pod as luminous body and CeO2/NiCo-LDH@Au complex as a co-reaction accelerator was proposed. A schematic diagram was shown in Scheme 1 . Two mechanisms enhanced the generation of ROS. On the one hand, NiCo-LDH provided a large number of active sites to promote electron transfer, on the other hand, the addition of CeO2 accelerated the formation of superoxide free radicals. The fabricated sensing platform demonstrated remarkable sensitivity and stability in detecting trenbolone (TB). Consequently, this approach paved new avenues for applications in environmental monitoring.
Materials and apparatus were found in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs and CeO2/NiCo-LDH@Au complex
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs and CeO2/NiCo-LDH@Au complex synthesis process were obtained in detail from the Supplementary material.

PANI was prepared by conventional method [30]. 1 mL of aniline and 1 mL of o-xylene were mixed. 0.18 g of APS was added to 10 mL of HCl (1 mol/L). Then, aniline/o-xylene mixture was slowly added to the above solution under stirring for 2h and the solution appeared as a dark green grout. The solution was washed with deionized water at 10,000 rpm for 5 min and dried at 60 °C for 24 h. The dark green solid was obtained for subsequent use.

The PANI-derived lotus-pod nano-hybrids were synthesized by hydrothermal method. 30 mg of previously obtained solid and 30 mg of N-CDs were added to 30 mL DMF and stirred for 30 min. The reaction was performed in a hydrothermal synthesis reactor at 180 °C for 8 h. The products were filtered through 0.22 μm Millipore membranes to remove large-sized impurities. Subsequently, they were dialyzed in bags with a molecular weight cut-off of 500 Da for 12 h. Finally, the dialysate in the bag was freeze-dried and the product was named PANI/N-CDs.

The preparation process of PANI/N-CDs-Ab2 was as follows: 500 μL of EDC, 100 μL of Ab2 were mixed with 2.0 mL PANI-N-CDs and oscillated at 4 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the unbound Ab2 was removed by centrifugation and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface was treated first. Then, CeO2/NiCo-LDH@Au was dropped onto the GCE and incubated at 4 °C for 1 h. Next, the capture antibody Ab1 was added dropwise and incubated for 1 h. Subsequently, non-specific site binding was blocked with 6 μL (1 wt%) of BSA. Subsequently, the electrode surface was modified with a series of different concentrations of TB and kept at a constant temperature to dry. After constructing the sensor, it was further modified at its interface with PANI/N-CDs-Ab2 and then placed at 4 °C for use.

The microstructure of the PANI/N-CDs composite has been comprehensively characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, N-CDs have a uniform elliptical nanoparticle morphology with sizes ranging from 1 to 10 nm. As shown in Fig. 1C and D, it was observed that the PANI/N-CDs composites exhibited an overall circular morphology. Therefore, in this paper, the PANI/N-CDs composites synthesized had a unique 'lotus pod' structure, in which N-CDs served as the 'lotus seeds' embedded within a 'lotus pod' framework composed of a polyaniline polymer matrix [30]. The subsequent comprehensive analysis of the crystal structure of PANI/N-CDs was conducted using X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. S1A, XRD patterns exhibited an obvious diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.2°, corresponding to the (200) plane of PANI.

Fig. S1B showed the Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) of PANI/N-CDs. The amide bond within the 1640 cm−1 band in PANI/N-CDs underwent a significant shift, serving as an indicator for assessing the degree of electron delocalization. The peak changes were attributed to the presence of van der Waals forces between the polyaniline-derived structure and N-CDs, which was consistent with previously reported literature [16,31].

Additionally, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the elemental valence characteristics of PANI/N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the PANI/N-CDs composite was composed of C, N, and O elements, all of which exhibited the 1 s valence state. As shown in Fig. 2B, the detailed analysis of C 1s indicated C–C/C=C (284.38 eV), C–N (285.58 eV), and C=N (287.28 eV), which provided crucial clues for understanding the chemical structure of the material. In the high-resolution spectra of N 1s in Fig. 2C, three distinct bonding states were revealed: the peaks at 398.08 eV, 399.48 eV, and 400.98 eV corresponded to the presence of quinolinamine, aniline groups, and nitrogen cation radicals, respectively. Fig. 2D showed the binding energy data of O 1s, in which there were two peaks of hydroxyl carbon–oxygen (532.08 eV) and carbonyl carbon–oxygen (531.38 eV).

In addition, the comprehensive analysis of N-CDs was carried out using Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) and Fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy. Fig. S2 showed a typical π–π* transition was observed in the range of 190–250 nm, which was attributed to the unique spherical structure of N-CDs. Simultaneously, an n–π* transition existed within the range of 300–370 nm, intimately related to the hydrazine-based structure of the N-CDs. Moreover, Fig. S3 exhibited the pronounced fluorescence properties of N-CDs in the blue and visible light regions, with the maximum excitation wavelength being 405 nm (indicated by the pink line) and the maximum emission wavelength being 457 nm (indicated by the violet line). These findings laid a key foundation for further elucidation of its optical properties.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images visually represented the micro-morphology of the CeO2/NiCo-LDH composites. As depicted in Fig. 1E, CeO2 assumed nanospheres morphology. Concurrently, Fig. 1F exhibited a distinct nanoflower-like morphology for NiCo-LDH. As shown in Fig. 1G, CeO2 nanospheres were evenly dispersed on the surface of NiCo-LDH nanoflowers and provided more attachment sites for Au NPs. Meanwhile, Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of the CeO2/NiCo-LDH@Au distributes of elements in Fig. S4. All the above results confirmed the successful synthesis of CeO2/NiCo-LDH@Au complex.

As shown in Fig. S5, the XRD patterns exhibited the crystal structures of NiCo-LDH and CeO2/NiCo-LDH. The diffraction peaks of NiCo-LDH aligned precisely with the standard JCPDS: 25–1363 [32], confirming that NiCo-LDH possessed a highly ordered layered structure. The characteristic peaks of CeO2/NiCo-LDH matched those of CeO2 (JCPDS: 34–0394) and NiCo-LDH, indicating that the composite had the crystal structure of both materials and was characterized by narrow and sharp peaks, directly reflecting its high crystallinity [33].

The XPS was executed to analyze the elemental composition and valence state distribution of the composites. As shown in Fig. S6, the XPS full spectrum of the composite indicated the presence of key elements such as C, O, Ce, Ni, and Co. Fig. 3 A showed the high-resolution spectrum of Ni 2p, with two peaks at 884.49 eV and 875.63 eV corresponding to Ni 2p1/2, and another two peaks at 863.39 eV and 858.22 eV corresponding to Ni 2p3/2. As shown in Fig. 3B, there were three peaks for O 1s associated with Adsorbed O (533.08 eV), Ovacancy (531.78 eV), and Metal–O, respectively. In Fig. 3C, Ce exhibited two valence states, namely Ce 3d5/2 (at 875.17 eV and 884.32 eV) and Ce 3d3/2 (at 900.92 eV and 919.06 eV). As shown in Fig. 3D, the peaks at 786.10 eV and 800.20 eV corresponded to Co2+, those at 798.90 eV and 783.70 eV corresponded to Co3+, and the peak at 776.10 eV corresponded to Co0. Additionally, two satellite peaks were present at 805.50 eV and 788.60 eV.

Based on the above studies, the sensing mechanism of the biosensor was shown in Fig. 4 . PANI/N-CDs were oxidized at the electrode surface to produce PANI/N-CDs+ (Eq. (1)). CeO2/NiCo-LDH acted as a co-reaction accelerator to promote the reaction process. NiCo (III), as the active metal center, gained electrons at the electrode surface to generate NiCo (II) with oxidizing property (Eq. (2)) [34]. CeO2 gained electrons at the electrode surface to generate Ce3+ with oxidizing property (Eq. (3)) [35]. And the oxidizing intermediate in the reaction reduces hydrogen peroxide to O2
− (Eqs. (4), (5)). O2
− interacted with PANI/N-CDs+ to produce PANI-CDs* excited state (Eq. (6)), which returned to the ground state to generate an ECL signal (Eq. (7)).
(1) PANI/N-CDs − e− → PANI/N-CDs+
(2) NiCo (III) + e− → NiCo (II)
(3) Ce4+ + e− → Ce3+
(4) NiCo (II) + H2O2 → NiCo (III) + O2
−
(5) Ce3+ + H2O2 → Ce4+ + O2
−
(6) PANI/N-CDs+ + O2
− → PANI/N-CDs*
(7) PANI/N-CDs* → PANI/N-CDs + hν

As shown in Fig. S8, the successful layer-by-layer modification of the biosensor was verified through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and cyclic voltammetry (CV). When only a GCE was used, there were no components on the electrode surface that hindered electron transfer, resulting in a strong redox current. Upon modification with CeO2/NiCo-LDH@Au, electron transfer was impeded, resulting in increased resistance (R et) and decreased redox current. When Ab1, BSA, and TB were sequentially modified onto the surface, the value of R et significantly increased while the redox current decreased progressively. After further modification of PANI/N-CDs-Ab2, the R et and redox current maintained previous trends. The above results indicated the successful construction of the biosensor.

The performance of the biosensor was investigated by testing a series of standard samples with different concentrations of TB ranging from 0.1 pg/mL to 50 ng/mL under optimal conditions to evaluate the performance in Fig. 5 A. By analyzing the biosensor shown in Fig. 5B, the resulting linear equation for the calibration curve: I ECL = 4382 + 1664 lg c, R2 = 0.980, and limit of detection (LOD) of 68.4 fg/mL. As shown in Table S1, the constructed biosensor exhibited a lower detection limit, suggesting broad application prospects for the accurate measurement of TB.

Stability was crucial for the practical application of biosensors [36,37]. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the biosensor demonstrated outstanding stability after continuous scanning over 12 cycles. To further evaluate the storage stability of the biosensor, it was stored and tested at 4 °C for 28 days (Fig. 6B). The results showed that the ECL signal remained relatively stable on the whole with RSD = 1.53 %, thus confirming its good long-term stability. Simultaneously, Fig. 6C showed that the ECL response of the five constructed biosensors was basically the same, and the RSD was 0.77 %, which indicated that the biosensor had good repeatability. As shown in Fig. 6D, when assessing the selectivity of the biosensor by using Diethylstilbestrol (DES), Progesterone (P), and Estradiol (E2) as interfering substances mixed with TB, the results indicated that the biosensor produced ECL signal comparable to that observed in the presence of TB alone. However, The ECL signal almost disappeared without the addition of TB, indicating the high selectivity of the biosensor.

The feasibility of the biosensor was verified in water samples from Jiazi Lake, University of jinan, using the standard addition method [38,39]. The water sample was first pretreated to remove suspended solids and then different concentrations of TB (0.53, 1.26 ng/mL) were added to the water sample. In Table S2, the recoveries of the assay were 96.8 %–101.9 % with RSD not higher than 3.8 %. The results indicated that the biosensor had good accuracy and were suitable for the trace detection of real domestic water.

In summary, a novel ECL biosensor was constructed for the detection of TB by using CeO2/NiCo-LDH as the co-reaction accelerator to promote the ECL response of PANI/N-CDs. PANI/N-CDs exhibited a distinctive and stable “lotus seedpod” structure and were used as an ECL probe with good conductivity and stable ECL signal output. In addition, CeO2/NiCo-LDH acted as a co-reaction accelerator, catalyzing the generation of O2
− from H2O2 and thus significantly enhancing the ECL intensity. The biosensor constructed had excellent performance and had potential applications in the prevention of environmental pollution.

Yanshuo Hao: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Huan Wang: Validation, Formal analysis. Caiyu Wang: Visualization, Investigation. Baojian Zhang: Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Hongmin Ma: Methodology, Investigation. Dan Wu: Methodology. Xiang Ren: Methodology. Yuyang Li: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Qin Wei: Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51904114 and 22274062), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2020M671982), Postdoctoral Creative Foundation of Shandong Province (No. 202002022), Postdoctoral Foundation in University of Jinan (No. 1003866), Doctoral Foundation in University of Jinan (No. XBS2415).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112761.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
